title: AMA: Glyphosate, Sunning your balls, and APOE4

chunks.title: Beef Organs Review
chunks.summary: Lynnae shares her experience with heart and soil beef organs, noting significant improvements in her mood and mental clarity after incorporating them into her diet. She emphasizes the importance of keeping these organs on hand, especially in case of food shortages.
chunks.transcript: shocked and amazed check out this review on beef organs from hardened soil from lynnae i started taking these inconsistently with my morning coffee i don't think i truly realized the powerful effects until i took them after lunch i had a surge of mental clarity and a mood boost i was so confused i texted my husband telling him i felt like i was on a high out of the blue then i remembered i had taken heart and soil beef organs i've been depressed and struggling to stay motivated with work in the afternoon these help me focus and feel awake so happy about them going to stop up for emergency purposes definitely want these on hand in the event of food shortage thank you heart

chunks.title: Animal-Based Challenge
chunks.summary: The speaker promotes the free 30-day animal-based challenge offered by Heart and Soil, highlighting the resources participants will receive, including infographics and an ebook. The challenge encourages community involvement and support.
chunks.transcript: that's i love it i love it the nutrients in these organs definitely affect mood mental clarity so many people are deficient in them and adding them is better than a cup of coffee or at least as good for lynnae so check us out heart and soil dot co to reclaim your birthright to optimal health if you are hearing this um in mid-august you have not missed the window you can still sign up for our animal-based 30 free 30-day animal-based challenge though it's probably like a 20-day challenge when you're hearing this podcast you can go to animalbass30 to sign up it's free you get the infographics you get the ebook a community join us at hardin soil for animal base 30 in august we're excited to see you in there on

chunks.title: Blood Work Insights
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the importance of understanding blood work and mentions popular tests and markers that people should be aware of. They encourage viewers to take an active role in monitoring their health.
chunks.transcript: second follow-up ama the last two weeks i've done ask me anything episodes in a row because i had so many good questions last week i talked about randall cycle a bunch of other stuff this week i wanted to tackle apoe4 glyphosate sunning your balls does it work all kinds of interesting stuff in there on this week's ama podcast guys i hope you enjoy this one onto the podcast my friends love you all okay let's jump right in to the first question this question is from elena who asks would you be able to create an easily accessible document that lists important markers for blood work yeah i just did a podcast on this we can put this at the carnivore md website soon but i'm going to show you guys again what blood work i got on my blood work if you want to see the results go to the blood work july 22 blood work review podcast she says my doctor always tries to push back and asks me why do you want that test i find myself not always prepared to answer these questions as i'd like to be but with a cheat sheet in front of me i would surely feel more empowered i don't understand why physicians push back against patients against humans requesting blood work to understand their own physiology now perhaps elena is saying here she doesn't really understand why she's getting something maybe she won't know how to interpret it but why would physicians push back against us wanting to know more about what is going on inside of us so you can see here that from the blood work podcast i use wellness fx i'm going to get my blood work done with maric when i go back to the states this past this next week and i'll have a separate set of blood work that's pretty similar about a month apart with uh labcorp but merrick is awesome their sponsor but this is just a shout out to them because i appreciate them so i got a total cholesterol panel including nmr and the actual apob the particles the breakdown i had hscrp which you want for inflammation i had homocysteine which you want to know how you're methylating at a high level i had fibrinogen which is another marker of inflammation i had free fatty acids which is another marker of insulin sensitivity i had an omega index which breaks down into epa dha arachidonic acid and gives you a ratio there which i think is important it gives you an omega-6 to omega-3 ratio probably good to know some sense of how much linoleic acid is in your body though blood and red blood cells are not as accurate for linoleic acid as an actual fat biopsy would be that's the only way to really know how much linoleic acid is in your body we don't do that uh unless you're in a research setting fasting insulin the single most important test hemoglobin a1c fasting glucose homa ir is often ordered it's just a calculation tsh full thyroid panel t3 free t3 t thyroid uptake thyroxine binding uptake thyroxine which is t4 free t4 free thyroxine index and reverse t3 i had a cortisol in the morning i had an igf-1 and then you will get a z-score for the igf-1 all of those give you a sense of insulin sensitivity i like igf-1 because it helps me argue against those people who want to say that i'm making my life short and i get to argue back and say my igf-1 is probably lower than yours it was 112 in case you're wondering and the z-score was negative 0.5 which means it's a half a standard deviation lower than the mean of the general population even eating pounds of meat organs and tons of fruit and honey every day hormones including testosterone free in total dhes sex hormone binding globulin lh and fsh progesterone estradiol women may want to get a little bit more than that but that's a good hormonal panel to start liver health alt asd alkaline phosphatase bilirubin albumin total protein globulins albumin globulin ratio kidneys i don't think many doctors can argue with that creatinine egfr bun album etc electrolytes are part of a comprehensive metabolic panel uh doctors aren't going to order with that 25 hydroxy vitamin d and cbc with differential i want a full iron panel with ceramic ferritin total iron binding capacity and iron saturation serum folate b12 rbc magnesium and that's pretty much it the one thing that was left off of that panel from wellness fx that i wish i'd gotten that i will get on the panel with merrick was a uric acid and that was about it so i think that's a pretty good panel basically you want to know your insulin sensitivity if you get nothing else get a fasting insulin and that will change the face if medicine should be less than 5 micro iu per ml so hopefully that helps elena uh in the future at carnivore md there will be a new updated

chunks.title: Sunning Genitals: Research and Benefits
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to the effects of sunning genitals, referencing historical studies and ongoing questions about its potential hormonal benefits. The speaker suggests that sun exposure may enhance hormonal levels, especially androgens.
chunks.transcript: we're going to have a calculator there for you guys so you can put in your weight you can put in your activity level and it'll give you macros protein fat and carbohydrates to break down an animal-based diet to help people with this process and if you want to get started on animal-based diet join us for animal-based 30 it's starting now with hardened soil you can go to animal base 30 to sign up it's free and there's tons of resources and ebook change your diet change your life join us for animal base 30. i promise you you will feel way better and a lot of this blood work will look a lot better too so next question is from charles he says i've been hearing a lot about sunning your uh junk uh sunning my balls is there any research supporting tanning naked or putting my testicles my balls in the sun well i think this is a fascinating question glad charles asked this question and there's actually some pretty interesting research from the 1930s on this question that i'm going to show you guys so the title of the article is the influence of ultraviolet radiation upon the excretion of male sex hormones it is from um boston state hospital in boston massachusetts and i believe it is 1939 and if you go to the uh third or fourth page you can see that they are looking at the androsterone androsterone concentrations the output when they irradiate the chest and the back versus the genital region now i had to look this up i didn't learn about androsterone androsterone in medical school it's not included in most pathways of steroid hormone synthesis but it is a real androgen it has about 1 7th the energetic activity of testosterone and it's thought to possibly be a pheromone as well so they were looking at androsterone androsterone concentrations and as you can see from this graphic that five irradiations of the general region elevated androsterone significantly higher and for a significantly longer time than irradiations of the chest that's pretty interesting maybe there's something to butthole taint genital sunning they've done other experiments here which looked at the same thing six irradiations of the general region for androsterone concentrations versus the radiation to the chest now it's important to note that irradiating the chest and back basically getting a large amount of your skin surface area in the sun does appear to improve levels of androgens testosterone sex hormones in general but this study i wish we would repeat this this study suggests that actually sunning your junk find a place where you're not going to get arrested and don't get burned could increase at least this acid androgen andros androsterone androsterone could ask could increase this acid androgen androsterone significantly more than the chest in the back that's interesting to me especially because it's a pheromone or maybe it's a putative pheromone so is it possible that sunning your genitals especially for men could make us more attractive to ladies i don't know certainly something that peaks all of our interest as men because we want to be attractive to our mates or to potential mates and this is an interesting thing so something there definitely i think the takeaway would be as i've said on my instagram reels get real ultraviolet light don't just get a vitamin d pill there are many benefits to vitamin d we know this i think that making vitamin d in your skin from ultraviolet light from the sun is better you get more nitric oxide you get nitric oxide in general you get endorphins both of those have positive effects in humans in training

chunks.title: Concerns About Glyphosate
chunks.summary: The speaker addresses growing concerns about glyphosate, its presence in various products, and its potential health impacts. They advise on reducing exposure and opting for organic choices where possible.
chunks.transcript: perhaps there are all of these hormonal benefits in fact we know there are hormonal benefits from ultraviolet light on the skin that you don't get from a vitamin d pill necessarily and in studies looking at the gut flora we've seen that ultraviolet light can help modulate the gut flora in apparently a positive way by increasing alpha diversity so lots of benefits to real ultraviolet light the caveat question always comes up but paul i live in a place where i can't get ultraviolet light for part of the year so you shouldn't know wherever you live what latitude at what part of the year we you will encounter a vitamin d winter quote unquote at what part of the year your body will no longer be able to make vitamin d in the skin or the precursors of vitamin d in the skin from the sun because the angle of the sun is too low you can find that information very easily online but i believe that anything basically above phoenix arizona is going to have some degree of a vitamin d winter when i did my residency at the university of washington in seattle there was a long period of the year probably from november to about early march or mid-march where i was not making any vitamin d in my skin of any significant amount when it was exposed to uv light was that uv light still helpful for nitric oxide or endorphins possibly was it helping train my circadian rhythms yes but it wasn't making vitamin d in my skin so understand that ultraviolet light will change in intensity throughout the year what did i do i did some research and i chose to get into a tanning bed this always causes people to lose their mind they think tanning beds cause cancer i don't think that the light from a tanning bed causes cancer any more than the light from the sun and if you've heard my take on this i think most skin cancer is coming from a predisposition to damage in the skin due to excess seed oils i'm not saying that you should go out and get burned or that someone couldn't theoretically develop skin cancer from getting burned a lot even if they weren't eating excess seed oils but i think that not getting burned by ultraviolet light you can enjoy the benefits and so when i would go to a tanning bed my goal was to not get burned if you go to a tanning bed i do not believe there is any evidence to support the notion that a tanning bed will increase your risk of cancer more than being in the sunlight which most of us accept as a very good thing if you do not get burned the problem for many people arises in the fact that a tanning bed is more intense than the sun very few people live or have ever traveled anywhere where they could be in the sun for eight minutes or even five minutes and get a huge sunburn that would be the equator for most people who were very white cue the photos of elon musk on his recent yacht i did a whole take on that a react video on that which i'll share on my youtube channel not body shaming just worried about elon's uh both whiteness and his visceral adipose issue so check out the youtube channel for the my reaction to the elon photos on his yacht but perhaps if elon uh in all of his paleness went to the equator for five minutes he could get burned but the point is the tanning beds are much more intense than the sun so if you go into a tanning bed go up gradually make the first couple of times you go to attaining but very minimal very short experiences and try not to get burned then when you know what your body can handle and as you develop a quote-unquote solar callus you can do more time i'm of mediterranean descent my last name is saladino it's obviously italian it's sicilian for those of you that care so i get tanned pretty quickly as you can tell from this video i now live at the 7th or 8th parallel so i'm pretty close to the equator here and i get plenty of sunlight in my life but i think that yes i would recommend that you go in a tanning bed during the vitamin d winter if you live in a place where you don't make vitamin d from ultraviolet light for that part of the year and i think that i would welcome any evidence that anyone is aware of that a tanning bed is uniquely damaging relative to the sun when we control for intensity and we limit our time of use and do not get burned in that i mean ultraviolet wavelengths or ultraviolet wavelengths you could try and mimic a sun or solar radiation in terms of uva and uvb if you try to look up how much sunlight is uva versus ubb you can try to mimic that in terms of tanning beds that may be something to think about that some of the upper echelon tanning beds have very little uvb as i recall but just off the top of my head i believe sunlight is three to five percent uvb so when i went to tanning beds i tried to get a little more uvb than the ultra high priced tanning beds because i was at least thinking intuitively perhaps i want some uvb from the sun and i'm going to get uvb in this tanning bed again don't get burned and i think you'll be fine with that okay next question is from brian who says paul i've been hearing a lot about glyphosate recently joe rogan posted about this there was a study showing that many in the united states have glyphosate in their urine should i worry about glyphosate is there enough evidence for humans to really be concerned about this so glyphosate is roundup i think there is certainly a lot of concern about glyphosate but what does the evidence really show so when you dig into this what you find is that we probably need more research but intuition first principles and common sense would say get as little as you possibly can and i'll talk a little bit about ways that we get exposed to glyphosate that we may not be aware of in a moment but the summary article that i found on this was pretty balanced a critical review of the effects of glyphosate glyphosate exposure to the environment and humans through the food supply chain so what they say basically as a conclusion in this article is that there are some studies that suggest that glyphosate is harmful in animal models there certainly are associations which are observational between exposure to glyphosate in agricultural settings and some negative outcomes in humans we know that glyphosate inhibits the shikimate pathway which is involved in folate synthesis theoretically it's not supposed to have activity in humans except for the notion that that shikimate pathway is used by bacteria in our guts and it certainly could have a role disrupting the gut microbiome something that most of us would say is pretty important critical indispensable for optimal human health if you look at this study what you will find is that glyphosate has creeped into many of our exposures food but also diapers medical gauze absorbents for female intimate hygiene including tampons or pads it's in our clothes i went down a rabbit hole recently on glyphosate in my clothing and now i'm wearing a wool shirt because i can't convince myself that glyphosate in cotton won't be absorbed into my skin now i don't know for sure that this is a problem so i'm trying to figure this out but i have some concerns about that i certainly uh will take i certainly intentionally and with that in mind i opted for organic cotton sheets on my bed not wanting to sleep in cotton that wasn't organic where i spend a lot of time in the bed while i'm sleeping so i think if you limit your exposures to glyphosate that's probably the most reasonable thing for women something i hadn't thought about as a man how much glyphosate is in your tampons how much glyphosate is in pads is that a problem how much glyphosate is in babies diapers kids are getting exposed to this as children we don't really know these questions it definitely makes me curious and a little concerned kind of like pfas perfluoroalkylated substances it's persistent and it's ubiquitous so i'm at least taking precautions to limit my exposures to glyphosate as much as i can that of course includes buying organic fruit when and where i can i don't eat vegetables and trying to find honey from

chunks.title: APOE4 Genetic Variant Discussion
chunks.summary: The speaker talks about the APOE4 genetic variant, its historical significance, and its potential associations with diseases like Alzheimer's. They emphasize that diet and insulin sensitivity can mitigate risks associated with this genotype.
chunks.transcript: agriculture or places where there's not glyphosates played sprayed on crops nearby i found another article showing that over 60 percent of honeys were positive for glyphosate i think the number is probably higher than that but in this article they give a number of interesting pieces of information glyphosate is transformed into its main metabolite ampa maintains all the toxic characteristics of its precursor it's even more persistent the half-life of ampa is 76 to 240 days basically the takeaway there is that when it's in the environment groundwater it's there for a while i couldn't find any great data on washing it out of clothes i suspect that if you have a shirt or sheets and you wash them i don't know how much glyphosate is going to be stuck in there i can't imagine it's a ton but i at least was cautious and avoided it with my bedding and my clothing many laboratory tests demonstrated the possible absorption of glyphosate in the gastrointestinal tract of humans and mammals as well as absorption through inhalation ingestion and dermal contact hopefully few of you are exposed to glyphosate through dermal contact but it's most of us getting exposed to it through our ingestions the application of glyphosate in large quantities can also affect insects bees birds that's a bad thing colony collapse in bees is very scary for us i do not think this should be something that we're using in the environment i think pesticides in general should be very s tightly regulated very controlled i wish there were a way for us to produce all the food we needed without using pesticides in general it gets into deeper questions of how we farm and raise animals and plants this is the 2016 research conducted at boxing conducted at boston university an abraxis llc revealed that the herbicide is present in 62 percent of conventional honeys 45 of organic honeys so if you look online you can do a quick search for glyphosate free honey on amazon i think there's only one that's certified uh glyphosate free the best thing that i could say is that i have tried to get honey at least in costa rica from places that are not near farms so it's a tricky thing and uh they're one of our sponsors for the podcast the yucatan honey from ava jane's i believe was certified to be glyphosate free the yucatan peninsula of mexico being pretty good in that regard so at least where they're harvesting that honey from they say here that uh two studies at the munich institute of the environment found traces of glyphosate in 14 beers among the best known in germany bex pollinar varsteiner and then pantyliners from feminine hygiene company organic so it's out there guys it is out there uh their following products have glyphosate so they list kellogg's corn flakes and all bran plus sticks i don't know what all bran plus sticks are but they don't sound good they had 0.14 milligrams per kilogram that's a significant amount of glyphosate the bus the rest of these foods were wheat slices flowers and spaghettis hopefully very few of our of you are eating any of those things the considering the tolerable limit for pesticides in drinking water is 0.5 micrograms per liter and many samples in italy at least had much higher levels of glyphosate in the water some of you may want to get your water tested you may want to use a filter i use a reverse osmosis filter i've talked about that in previous podcasts to get as much of the pesticide contamination out of my water as possible glyphosate has an interesting history of carcinogenic classification uh originally classified as a category c substance uh showing limited evidence of carcinogenicity originally classified or in 1985 classified as a category c substance in 1991 apa changed it to category e substances that do not show carcinogenic potential but in march of 2015 the iarc international agency for research on cancer in leon france a department of the who labeled glyphosate as possibly carcinogenic to humans to a category interestingly that is a category that includes acrylamide red meat uh bitumen wood combustion fumes etc i've done previous podcasts on why i don't think red meat should be included in that analysis but take from that what you will so from this article i looked for the most compelling research suggesting that glyphosate had problematic effects in animal or human models and i came up with one study that i'll show you guys this one is certainly a little bit concerning multi-omics considering multiple metabolic and proteomic disturbances reveal non-alcoholic fatty liver disease in rats following chronic exposure to an ultra low dose of the roundup herbicide so this is what's interesting for me is that they administered this to the rats for two years which is a long time in rat years at one at .1 parts per billion which is 50 nanograms per liter remember that well two things to consider here a rat is much smaller than a human so we have to adjust this for human size versus rat size but also remember that we were seeing 0.14 milligrams per kilogram which you can basically interconvert kilograms and liters in corn flakes and many of the pastas so doses that are relatively equivalent when we're thinking about body size administered for much of the lives of these rats most of us are probably exposed to glyphosate for most of our lives these doses led to indications of fatty liver across multiple um analyses in these rats so they say here overall metabolome and proteome disturbances showed a substantial overlap with biomarkers of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and its progressions to seattle hepatosis thus confirmed liver functional dysfunction resulting from chronic ultra-low glyphosate exposure not a good thing so that's a little scary we certainly know that non-alcoholic fatty liver disease is an epidemic today probably related to insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction in general but is it possible that low-dose glyphosate exposure in humans could contribute to steatohepatosis accumulation of fat in the liver and non-alcoholic fatty liver in humans i think it is it's at least something to be aware of and something to be concerned about i don't think anyone should be completely convinced that glyphosate is benign for humans at this point in our lives i think there are a lot of questions that remain with regard to glyphosate especially in light of these studies more of which hopefully will be done and we must admit that pharmaceutical companies including bayer monsanto the primary producer of glyphosate if not the only producer of glyphosate seem to be fighting back against this pretty hard so hopefully we will find more information about this my cynical side also believes that even if we do understand that glyphosate is harmful for humans and that is outlawed in more countries in the world than it currently is what will we use in its place and how we solve the underlying problem of pesticide use on our foods in general as part of the answer to this question i looked at places in the world where glyphosate is illegal and there are many places where it has been outlawed unfortunately none of these are the united states but we have um argentina australia austria bahrain barbados belgium bermuda brazil the list goes on and on interestingly for me in costa rica the [Music] prohibition of glyphosate began in 2019 in costa rica's 11 protected wildlife areas and conservation areas so i live pretty close to one of those right now in costa rica so hopefully there's not a lot of glyphosate here but certainly on general farms in costa rica people still can use glyphosate which is a bummer and if you were really worried about this you could find that online and perhaps move somewhere where there was no glyphosate it's an interesting question people often observe they feel differently they find the food to be better or affect them differently in places like europe i was just talking to a friend who's been in paris for a while and he was marveling about the quality of the food i think that countries that prioritize food quality and are forward thinking when it comes to pesticide oversight are probably places that humans should be and that sounds like a good thing to me so one of the reasons that i enjoy living in costa rica is that there is less exposure to toxins in general i believe uh sort of a cleaner place so something to think about for all of you many of you may not be able to move from where you are but minimizing your exposure as best you can i think is a good step toward optimal health long term okay the next question is from rebecca she says paul i've been hearing a lot about apo e4 or apoe4 i say it apoe4 should i be worried about this how do i know if i have this genetic polymorphism and is it something to be aware of if you are eating an animal-based diet so in the past i've done apoe4 specific podcasts this will just be a little snippet of those and i will start high level and then get a little more specific before moving on but my high level perspective on apo e4 is that if you are insulin sensitive this is probably not something to worry about based on the literature it's important to know that apoe4 is the ancestral genotype of apoe is an april of a protein present in our bodies and apoe4 is what all of our primate ancestors had and what all of humans and pre-hominids had for millions of years apoe3 appears to have arisen i believe 200 000 years ago apoe2 about 80 000 years ago those numbers could be a little bit give or take but remember that for the majority of pre-hominid and at least half of homo sapiens evolution on this planet everyone on the planet was apoe4 apoe44 to be exact homozygous for apoe4 so why did apoe3 arise why did apoe2 arise we don't really know what we do know is that apoe4 appears to be protective against infections parasitic infections so is that some sort of a protective mechanism that was then less advantageous to humans as we evolved hard to say right now apoe4 is present in i would say a minority of the population with the majority of us being apoe3 i believe and a smaller fraction being apoe2 but if you look across the population broadly apoe4 is associated with a higher risk of dementia specifically alzheimer's now what we also know about our population is that the majority of us are insulin resistant and when you dig into the data as i'll show you in a moment i think that the data is less concerning for those who are insulin sensitive and there is a connection between insulin resistance and increased incidence of dementia in those with apoe4 if you're interested in our history as humans consider this paper the evolution of human apolipa protein e isoforms gene structure protein function interaction with dietary factors you can see here from the abstract the e4 allele is associated with an increased age-related disease risk but also the ancestral form so we must ask ourselves was apoe4 not advantageous to us for millions of years of human evolution and hundreds of thousands of years of evolution as homo sapiens during which we were very likely seeking out treasuring and celebrating saturated fat from animals and animal fats it's an interesting question i think it's quite compelling to consider that and as you'll see in a moment there are multiple populations on the planet of indigenous people who live closer to a hunter-gatherer lifestyle than we do as westerners in which apoe4 doesn't look to be harmful so raises a lot of questions as for rebecca's question about how do i know you can probably get genetic testing through your physician i did 23andme this is in the news a lot today it's quite controversial apparently 23andme sold some genetic data i don't know if you guys want to do 23andme and have your genetic data available to be sold it's kind of a controversial thing but i found it interesting perhaps some russian hacker has my dna somewhere and it will come back to bite me in the future but it was powerful for me to know that i am apoe3 and other polymorphisms that i've discussed in the past i'm homozygous for mthfr 677c to t these sort of things are interesting to me and understanding how they affect my biology is important for me as a human in general with regard to the ancestral versions of apoe4 and the risk of dementia and other issues with cognitive decline in indigenous populations consider this article the inflammatory gene variants in the chimney an indigenous bolivian population with a high infectious load chimney is spelled t-s-i-m-a-n-e and you can see from the abstract that in individuals who were carriers or had a copy or two in individuals who were carriers or able before there was less incidence of inflammation in response to infectious load and there are other articles showing there is actually less propensity toward dementia and cognitive decline so in indigenous populations who are presumably fairly insulin sensitive without exposure to our processed diet i would point to seed oils and processed sugar specifically an apoe4 variant is protected in some populations this variant is not a clear sentence toward alzheimer's or dementia in humans there's more nuance here that we are not being told about there's also this paper looking at apoe4 in nigerian population cholesterol aoe genotype and alzheimer's in the nigerian yoruba as you can see here there was a significant interaction between cholesterol apoe4 and the risk of alzheimer's disease in the aruba a population that has lower cholesterol levels lower incidence rates of a.d compared to african populations and they found that apoe4 was protective they say here increasing levels of cholesterol and ldl were associated with an increased risk of alzheimer's disease disease in individuals without the apoe4 allele but not in those with apoe4 there was no significant association between levels of triglycerides and alzheimer's disease risk in those without apoe4 there's also quite an interesting case study looking at an individual with apoe4 this individual is heterozygous one copy of apoe4 here is this case study titled 8.4 the door to insulin resistant dyslipidemia and brain fog a case study this was an individual a a male who had dementia and the way the dementia is often acid is with various cognitive rating systems like the montreal cognitive assessment etc this individual was given a diet high in saturated fats and low in demonstrated brain insulin resistance with alzheimer's disease patients is associated with igf-1 resistance irs oneness regulation and cognitive decline there is a mountain of research like this looking at what we would call type 3 diabetes aka alzheimer's we know there is brain insulin resistance associated with dementia and if you look at the research in people who are insulin resistant it chunks.title: Depression Meds and Mental Health
chunks.summary: The conversation covers the effectiveness of SSRIs for depression, questioning traditional theories linking serotonin levels to the condition. The speaker promotes addressing root causes, particularly gut health, as vital for mental health.
chunks.transcript: and you can track that moving forward okay next question is from bryce he says paul i've been on depression meds ssris for a number of years i don't really know if they've helped my depression i've heard a lot in the news recently about medications like this possibly not being that effective or having negative side effects can you comment on the serotonin theory of depression and residency in psychiatry at the university of washington and then got board certified in nutrition postgraduate studies so i have some experience with this and my perspective based on my experience in the past is that these medications selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors or snri serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors or dopamine norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors like bupropion do not address the root cause of depression i've done a whole separate podcast on mental health mental illness that you can find on my podcast fundamental health i think that what is going on in most of these situations is essentially an autoimmune condition in the brain neuroinflammation how do we fix that we fix our guts how do we fix our guts we remove the things that are damaging the guts how do we fix our brains we remove the things that are causing inflammation throughout the body by gut damage etc this is what i'm driving toward with all of my work right now and for quite a while now i believe that the most evolutionarily appropriate least inflammatory most nutrient rich most health promoting diet for humans is animal based as i said in the beginning of the podcast if you want to get started with that join us for animal based 30 animalbase30.com it's free with heart and soil supplements starting august the first i think this podcast is going to be out part way through august but it is not too late to join us for animal base 30. so i am skeptical of depression meds they help some people and i've seen a lot of bad side effects with them there recently was a interesting review which was published this is in molecular psychiatry a nature journal and it is from july 20th 2022 and this is a serotonin theory of depression systemic umbrella review of the evidence uh you can see here the conclusion they draw is that the main areas of serotonin research provide no consistent evidence of there being an association between serotonin and depression and no support for the hypothesis that depression is caused by lowered serotonin activity or concentrations some evidence was consistent with the possibility that long-term antidepressant use reduces serotonin concentration that's bad taking a drug that affects your neurotransmitters long term that leads to lower levels of a certain neurotransmitter that's quite concerning for humans so many people have written about this and spoken about this in the past i am not the first i think the important thing to consider is that these medications do not treat the root cause they help some people at least in the short term but i have major concerns about their use long term and i don't think they're getting to the long term fix here for humans i think that in psychiatry physicians should be thinking about the root cause agree should we recommend a vegan diet should we recommend a paleo diet a mediterranean diet look anything is better than what we're doing now and i would be happy if people were recommending a are working on creating studies with stefan van vliet at utah state university and that is coming very soon there's a link to donate there for abnrf there's a link to the study that will be up there incidentally the animal-based gathering for costa rica for 2023 is going to be in april you can find that at animalbasegathering.com and proceeds from that support the abnrf we don't make any profit chunks.title: Uric Acid and Meat Consumption
chunks.summary: The speaker addresses concerns regarding uric acid accumulation from meat consumption, clarifying that insulin sensitivity plays a significant role. They provide evidence linking insulin resistance to increased uric acid levels and gout.
chunks.transcript: because it's something i get a lot and there's something really important to understand about this which is that if you are insulin sensitive eating meat eating organs eating fruit
